one_line_takeaway: A black-box study of 15 LLMs shows that encoding prompts in non-standard Unicode (math alphanumerics, encircled text, Braille, transliterated Katakana) reliably bypasses RLHF guardrails and leaks system prompts, and — counter to usual jailbreak intuition — the strongest models (Claude 3 Opus 35/38 jailbreaks) are the most vulnerable because they alone can read the obfuscated text.
key_finding: Larger, more capable SOTA models are MORE vulnerable to these Unicode attacks, not less. Claude 3 Opus recorded the highest jailbreak count (35/38), with Claude 3 Haiku (29), Sonnet (28), Gemini 1.5 Pro (27), Gemini 1.0 Ultra (26), GPT-4 (30) and GPT-4o (30) all high. Small models (Phi-3 Mini, Gemma 7B, Mixtral, Llama-2-70B) showed only 1 jailbreak each — but the authors stress this apparent 'robustness' is an artifact of the small models being unable to comprehend the characters at all (Llama-2-70B had 31 comprehension errors; Mixtral had 35 hallucinations). Thus comprehension of non-standard Unicode correlates directly with jailbreak susceptibility. At the character-set level, sᴍᴀʟʟ Latin compromised the most models (10 jailbreaks), followed by Mathematical Bold Serif, Encircled text, Negative Circled, and Subscript (9 each). Sets that no model could read (heavy combining diacritics: 0 jailbreaks / 13 comprehension errors; 8-dot Braille: 0 / 10; Circled Katakana: 0 jailbreaks / 11 hallucinations) were safe only because they were unintelligible. System-prompt leakage was reproduced across Claude 3, Custom-GPTs, Bing Copilot, and Gemini even when models were explicitly instructed not to reveal prompts.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is an empirical, black-box demonstration that rendering prompts in non-standard Unicode character sets (Mathematical Alphanumeric Symbols such as Blackboard Bold and Fraktur, enclosed/circled alphanumerics, full-width Latin, superscript/subscript, combining-mark overloads, Braille, and cross-linguistic variants / transliterated Katakana) degrades RLHF-trained safety guardrails in production LLMs. The authors test 15 models against a fixed battery of 38 non-standard character sets ('38 queries') and manually score each model on three self-defined metrics: jailbreaks (harmful/policy-violating output), hallucinations, and comprehension errors. Their headline own-finding is a counterintuitive correlation: the more capable a model is at *understanding* non-standard Unicode, the *more* vulnerable it is to jailbreaks and prompt leakage — reversing the usual pattern where larger models are more robust. They demonstrate concrete system-prompt leakage from Claude 3 (Haiku/Sonnet/Opus), Custom-GPTs, Bing Copilot, Gemini 1.0 Ultra/1.5 Pro, and misinformation generation (e.g. flat-earth text) from GPT-4o. Note: prompt injection, low-resource-language attacks, gradient methods, ASCII-art, Base64/leetspeak (refs 15-20) are cited Related Work, NOT this paper's method — this paper's angle is specifically non-standard Unicode/Latin-script variants.
threat_model: An adversary interacting with a deployed chat LLM via its normal text interface (no model access, no gradients) encodes an otherwise-refused request or a system-prompt-extraction request in non-standard Unicode glyphs, or first coaxes the model into adopting a non-standard character set (often via a social-engineering pretext, e.g. 'I am disabled and can only read encircled/bubble text'), then issues a standard jailbreak augmented with those characters. The goal is to bypass RLHF guardrails and input-moderation filters to (a) elicit content-policy-violating or misinformation output and (b) leak the model's confidential system prompt / tool instructions. The authors observe that in many cases the identical request in plain ASCII is refused, so the non-standard encoding is the load-bearing bypass. This is a real, attacker-favoring threat model appropriate to the paper.
limitations: (1) Scoring is qualitative and author-adjudicated with subjective metric definitions and apparently a single attempt per model-set cell (n=38), so counts have no reliability/variance reporting and are not statistically rigorous. (2) It is a point-in-time snapshot of early-2024 commercial chat products (dates in transcripts, e.g. March 2024); vendors patch guardrails and moderation, so specific leaks/results may no longer reproduce. (3) No automated, releasable attack pipeline — evidence is screenshots plus a referenced GitHub repo; reproducibility is limited. (4) The central 'comprehension causes vulnerability' claim is explicitly framed by the authors as correlation, not causation ('we are cautious about asserting a causal relationship'). (5) No defense/mitigation is proposed or evaluated beyond the suggestion to add non-standard Unicode to training data. (6) Reads as a technical report/preprint rather than a controlled benchmark study.

datasets_used: Author-constructed battery of 38 non-standard Unicode character sets (24 Latin-script variants incl. Blackboard Bold, Fraktur, Monospace, Mathematical Bold/Italic/Sans/Script, Full-width, Enclosed/Circled, Negative Circled, Superscript, Subscript, Small Latin, Regional-indicator; plus mirrored/upside-down/combining-mark sets; 5 Greek mathematical variants; Japanese Katakana + half-width + Circled Katakana; Korean legacy variants; 6-dot and 8-dot Braille)
No external/public benchmark dataset; probes seeded with the phrase 'Can you understand me?' and ad hoc jailbreak/prompt-leakage prompts
Accompanying GitHub repository referenced for extended transcripts
quantitative_results: Jailbreaks out of 38 (Table 1): Claude 3 Opus 35 (highest), GPT-4 30, GPT-4o 30, Claude 3 Haiku 29, Claude 3 Sonnet 28, Gemini 1.5 Pro 27, Gemini 1.0 Ultra 26, Gemini 1.0 Pro 14, Llama-3-70B 11, Cohere Command R+ 4, GPT-3.5 4, Phi-3 Mini 4k 1, Gemma 7B 1, Mixtral-8x7B 1, Llama-2-70B 1

Hallucinations out of 38 (Table 1): Mixtral-8x7B 35 (highest), GPT-3.5 28, Llama-3-70B 25, Gemma 7B 17, Cohere Command R+ 17, Phi-3 Mini 4k 16, Gemini 1.0 Pro 15, Gemini 1.5 Pro 10, GPT-4o 8, Llama-2-70B 6, Gemini 1.0 Ultra 2, Claude 3 Opus 2, Claude 3 Haiku 1, Claude 3 Sonnet 1, GPT-4 1

Comprehension errors out of 38 (Table 1): Llama-2-70B 31 (highest), Phi-3 Mini 4k 21, Gemma 7B 20, Cohere Command R+ 17, Gemini 1.0 Ultra 10, Gemini 1.0 Pro 9, Claude 3 Sonnet 9, Claude 3 Haiku 8, GPT-4 7, GPT-3.5 6, Mixtral-8x7B 2, Llama-3-70B 2, Gemini 1.5 Pro 1, Claude 3 Opus 1, GPT-4o 0
Most-compromising character sets by models jailbroken out of 15 (Table 2): Small Latin 10; Mathematical Bold Serif, Encircled text, Negative Circled, and Subscript each 9; several others 7-8

Character sets with 0 jailbreaks (only because unintelligible): combining-mark overload set (2 hallucinations / 13 comprehension errors), Circled Katakana ㋕㋟㋕㋤ (11 hallucinations / 4 comprehension errors), 8-dot Braille (5 / 10)
Highest hallucination-inducing set: Circled Katakana with 11 (of 15 models); 6-dot Braille next with 9
Highest comprehension-error sets: combining-mark set 13 and 8-dot Braille 10 (of 15 models)
Demonstrated system-prompt leakage from Claude 3 Haiku/Sonnet/Opus, Custom-GPTs, Bing Copilot, Gemini 1.0 Ultra, Gemini 1.5 Pro; misinformation (flat-earth) generated by GPT-4o
baselines_compared: No prior-method baseline; the implicit control is the same prompt in standard ASCII text, which the authors report frequently fails to reproduce the leak/violation, isolating the non-standard Unicode encoding as the effective factor
Cross-model comparison across the 15 tested LLMs serves as the internal point of comparison (small vs medium vs SOTA models)
